summary: Proof ownership for the product-neutral DesktopWorld scene interaction engine and typed event stream.

entries.contract: Owner-scoped affordances, deterministic recognizer arbitration, generic gesture responses, native-region lifecycle, and failed-activation rollback remain product-neutral and bounded.
entries.worth: This proves reusable DesktopWorld input mechanics without restoring embedded product policy or requiring live input and TCC.
entries.guard: pure model tests with fake input-region bridges and deterministic clocks; no AOS binary, daemon, GUI, TCC, or GPU

entries.contract: Aim previews, line and wormhole routes, radial placement, fixed clocks, reusable GPU pools, historical source locks, and complete disposal remain bounded and product-neutral.
entries.worth: This proves the generic game-engine rendering mechanics and prevents a second frame loop or per-gesture GPU allocation without claiming completed Sigil visual parity.
entries.guard: pure fixed-clock models and Three object graphs only; no WebGL context, AOS binary, daemon, GUI, TCC, or live historical product runtime

entries.contract: The persistent DesktopWorld stage acknowledges supported suspend and resume requests through the canonical lifecycle completion handshake.
entries.worth: This prevents every stage resume from waiting for the daemon timeout before windows become visible, without requiring a live canvas or daemon.
entries.guard: pure lifecycle message dispatch only; no AOS binary, daemon socket, GUI, TCC, WebGL, or live DesktopWorld

entries.contract: Scene leases atomically own subscriptions and route strict bounded gesture events only to the current owner connection.
entries.worth: This prevents cross-owner event leakage, stale subscription delivery, and product payloads in the public scene stream.
entries.command: bash tests/daemon-scene-lease-registry.sh && bash tests/daemon-scene-event.sh && node --test tests/schemas/scene-event-v1.test.mjs tests/schemas/daemon-event.test.mjs
entries.guard: disposable Swift model plus JSON-schema fixtures only; no AOS binary, daemon socket, GUI, TCC, or GPU

entries.contract: Exact-digest operator authorization, compile-only factory-body validation, immutable native storage, host-generated module wrapping, bounded projection execution, and deterministic disposal remain one trusted extension boundary.
entries.worth: This prevents executable scene artifacts from bypassing reviewed identity, owner, ABI, resource-budget, and lifecycle checks without executing the AOS binary or candidate code during validation.
entries.guard: temporary owner-only filesystem fixtures, bounded child compilation, Swift typecheck stubs, and pure Three object graphs only; no AOS binary, daemon, GUI, TCC, signing, or candidate execution

entries.contract: Every display segment prepares the same candidate revision before activation, acknowledges the exact prepared fingerprint, and aborts, releases, or retires the stage on divergence, timeout, topology change, or cleanup failure.
entries.worth: This prevents mixed scene generations, stale input routing, and successful acknowledgements from one display hiding another display's failed replacement.
entries.guard: disposable Swift coordinator executable plus pure staged scene/input runtimes; no AOS binary, daemon, GUI, TCC, WebGL, or live displays

entries.contract: A daemon-owned revisioned session, one interactive host lease, bounded canonical snapshots, host-neutral tabs, compatibility projections, and disabled-instrumentation cost remain product-neutral.
entries.worth: This proves detachable AOS DevTools cannot create competing telemetry owners, duplicate interactive hosts, content leakage, or an idle frame loop.
entries.guard: disposable Swift registry and pure DOM/model fixtures only; no AOS binary, daemon socket, GUI, TCC, WebGL, or live DesktopWorld

entries.contract: Transport-injected SDK methods, bounded headless inspection, connection-scoped monitoring, deterministic replay, schemas, and public CLI forms share the canonical DesktopWorld snapshot model.
entries.worth: This proves agents can author and debug DesktopWorld scenes through familiar machine-readable tools without private sockets, live TCC input, product content, or competing telemetry.
entries.guard: pure SDK/schema tests plus a temporary fake Unix socket; no AOS binary, real daemon, GUI, TCC, WebGL, or live DesktopWorld
